population: 814 laboratory-confirmed viral respiratory illnesses in healthcare personnel
hospital_acquired_definition: Healthcare personnel respiratory illness with prior-week exposure assessment
risk_factor_definition: Exposure to ill adult household members
finding_text: Adult ill household members were strongly associated with viral respiratory illness in healthcare personnel
key_limitations: Not patient-acquisition paper; mixed respiratory viruses
study_reported_implication: Household exposure was a major source of HCW viral illness
codeblue_translation: Model HCW household exposure as an upstream workforce-risk input

population: 814 laboratory-confirmed viral respiratory illnesses in healthcare personnel
hospital_acquired_definition: Healthcare personnel respiratory illness with prior-week exposure assessment
risk_factor_definition: Exposure to ill coworkers
finding_text: Exposure to ill coworkers was associated with viral respiratory illness in healthcare personnel
key_limitations: Not patient-acquisition paper; mixed respiratory viruses
study_reported_implication: Coworkers were an important source of HCW viral illness
codeblue_translation: Model coworker exposure as a workforce-spread input

population: 814 laboratory-confirmed viral respiratory illnesses in healthcare personnel
hospital_acquired_definition: Healthcare personnel respiratory illness with prior-week exposure assessment
risk_factor_definition: Exposure to ill social contacts outside household
finding_text: Exposure to ill social contacts was associated with viral respiratory illness
key_limitations: Not patient-acquisition paper; mixed respiratory viruses
study_reported_implication: Social contacts also contributed substantially to HCW viral illness
codeblue_translation: Model social exposure as an upstream workforce-risk input

population: 814 laboratory-confirmed viral respiratory illnesses in healthcare personnel
hospital_acquired_definition: Healthcare personnel respiratory illness with prior-week exposure assessment
risk_factor_definition: Direct patient contact
finding_text: Healthcare personnel with direct patient contact had higher odds of illness
key_limitations: Patient-facing status is broad and not influenza-specific
study_reported_implication: Direct patient contact modestly increased HCW illness risk
codeblue_translation: Use patient-facing work as a workforce exposure modifier

population: 814 laboratory-confirmed viral respiratory illnesses in healthcare personnel
hospital_acquired_definition: Healthcare personnel respiratory illness with prior-week exposure assessment
risk_factor_definition: Exposure to patients with recognized respiratory illness
finding_text: Exposure to patients with recognized respiratory illness was not associated with infection
key_limitations: May reflect effective PPE or unrecognized sources
study_reported_implication: Recognized sick patients were not the main signal in this HCW study
codeblue_translation: Do not over-weight only recognized patient exposure

population: 82 participants: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 doctors
hospital_acquired_definition: Confirmed influenza in geriatric short-stay unit
risk_factor_definition: Nurse role as dominant staff transmission vector
finding_text: Nurses appeared to transmit infection to patients more frequently than doctors
key_limitations: Unit-specific modeling study
study_reported_implication: Nurses were the primary staff-to-patient vector in this setting
codeblue_translation: Prioritize nurse-focused controls and staffing logic

population: 82 participants: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 doctors
hospital_acquired_definition: Confirmed influenza in geriatric short-stay unit
risk_factor_definition: Nurse-to-nurse influenza transmission
finding_text: Nurse-to-nurse transmission rate was 0.34 per day
key_limitations: Unit-specific modeling study
study_reported_implication: Staff-staff spread among nurses was relevant
codeblue_translation: Model nurse-to-nurse spread in workforce transmission logic

population: 152 HCWs and 542 inpatients over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Prospective surveillance study with sequencing/contact tracing
hospital_acquired_definition: Influenza infection in acute care during study monitoring
risk_factor_definition: Influenza-positive HCW shedding without symptoms
finding_text: HCWs regularly shed influenza virus without symptoms, but monitored asymptomatic transmission was rare
key_limitations: Low number of observed transmission events
study_reported_implication: Asymptomatic HCW shedding exists but did not generate observed monitored transmission here
codeblue_translation: Use as nuance in staff-vector policy logic

population: Five seasonal influenza outbreaks over 10 years
hospital_acquired_definition: Hospital outbreaks involving patients and healthcare workers
comparator_group: Outbreaks occurring earlier vs end of season
risk_factor_definition: Late-season outbreaks occurring >100 days after staff vaccination with healthcare-worker index cases
finding_text: Three of five outbreaks occurred at the end of March or beginning of April after >100 days from vaccination; the reported index case was a nurse or office worker in each, and >50% of those affected were healthcare workers
key_limitations: Short report with few outbreaks
study_reported_implication: The findings were consistent with intra-seasonal waning of vaccine immunity contributing to end-of-season healthcare-worker-linked outbreaks
codeblue_translation: Use late-season timing after staff vaccination as a staff-vector vigilance signal
tags: HCW INDEX; WANING IMMUNITY; END OF SEASON

population: Hospitalized short-stay inpatients with influenza-like illness; 24 confirmed HA influenza cases and 141 influenza-negative controls over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Case-case negative control study nested in a prospective cohort
hospital_acquired_definition: HA influenza defined as virologically confirmed influenza with symptom onset >=72 h after ward admission
comparator_group: Confirmed HA influenza cases vs influenza-negative ILI controls
risk_factor_definition: Ward-level influenza vaccine coverage among healthcare workers, especially coverage >=40%
finding_text: After adjustment, healthcare-worker influenza vaccination coverage >=40% was associated with reduced risk of hospital-acquired influenza in hospitalized patients; crude OR declined from 0.52 to 0.14 as ward coverage increased from 20% to 40%
adjustment_level: Mixed-effect logistic regression adjusted for patient sex, age, potential influenza source on ward in prior 5 days, ward type, and influenza season
clinical_context: Short-stay hospitalized patients under systematic PCR testing
key_limitations: Pilot study with limited sample size and only 24 HA influenza cases
study_reported_implication: HCW vaccination was highly suggestive of preventing HA influenza among hospitalized patients
codeblue_translation: Use ward HCW vaccination coverage as a staff-source protective modifier and prevention target
tags: HCW VACCINATION; SOURCE CONTROL; WARD COVERAGE

population: 1186 hospitalized influenza patients across five influenza seasons
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial influenza captured in hospital surveillance and compared before vs after strict staff mask policy
risk_factor_definition: Mandatory surgical mouth-nose protection for all HCWs during their full shift when at least three influenza patients were simultaneously present on a ward
finding_text: After introduction of strict staff masking, nosocomial influenza incidence fell nearly 50% and nosocomial mortality fell by 85%
clinical_context: Ward-level staff-source control during influenza season
key_limitations: Monocentric retrospective design with concurrent hand-hygiene improvement
study_reported_implication: Continuous staff masking appeared strongly protective against nosocomial influenza spread and death
codeblue_translation: Use strict ward-level staff masking as a high-priority staff-vector control measure when influenza burden rises

population: 278 healthcare workers with complete follow-up
acquisition_context: HCW infection burden relevant to nosocomial spread
hospital_acquired_definition: Not a patient HAI definition study; HCW influenza documented by PCR and/or seroconversion
risk_factor_definition: Laboratory-confirmed influenza in healthcare workers with absent or minimal symptoms
finding_text: Among 62 HCW with influenza, 46.8% were asymptomatic, 41.9% were paucisymptomatic, and only 11.3% were symptomatic
key_limitations: Most diagnoses were serologic rather than symptom-triggered PCR; patient transmission not directly measured
study_reported_implication: Silent or minimally symptomatic HCW likely represent an important hidden transmission reservoir
codeblue_translation: Use asymptomatic and paucisymptomatic HCW infection as a major reason to rely on universal staff precautions, not symptom-triggered precautions alone

setting: Short-stay acute care for the elderly unit
population: 82 individuals: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 medical doctors
study_design: Pilot stochastic transmission-model study using RFID contact data
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial transmissibility modeled from documented individual contact data and confirmed influenza cases
comparator_group: Transmission between patients, nurses and doctors
risk_factor_definition: Estimated transmission rate from nurses to patients compared with other staff categories
finding_text: Model-estimated transmission from nurses to patients was 1.04/day on average versus 0.38/day from medical doctors to patients; transmission between nurses was 0.34/day
adjustment_level: SEIR modeling with observed contact data
key_limitations: Pilot single-unit modeling study with small numbers
study_reported_implication: Nurses appeared to be the dominant staff group for patient-ward influenza transmission in this setting
codeblue_translation: Prioritize nurse-focused infection-control reinforcement and staffing-aware transmission review in vulnerable wards

population: 256 nosocomial influenza cases over 2480010 patient-days
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial influenza defined as laboratory-positive influenza diagnosed 7 days or more after admission
comparator_group: Monthly nosocomial influenza incidence vs HCW vaccination coverage
risk_factor_definition: Institutional clinical healthcare-worker influenza vaccination coverage
finding_text: In adjusted analysis, no statistically significant association was observed between clinical HCW vaccination coverage and nosocomial influenza incidence, although the effect direction suggested protection; a 10% increase in coverage corresponded to IRR 0.89
adjustment_level: Modified Poisson regression adjusted for background influenza activity and testing volume
key_limitations: Ecological design; non-significant association; timing/formulation issues may have weakened effect
study_reported_implication: HCW vaccination remained directionally protective but difficult to demonstrate statistically at institution level
codeblue_translation: Keep as lower-weight supportive evidence for HCW vaccination as a staff-source preventive measure

population: 106 HCWs with influenza or influenza-like illness; 234 hospitalized patients exposed
study_design: Descriptive epidemiological study with questionnaire and chart review
hospital_acquired_definition: Hospitalized patients exposed to HCWs with laboratory-confirmed influenza or ILI
diagnostic_method: Self-report plus clinical review and exposure follow-up
risk_factor_definition: Healthcare workers continuing to work despite influenza or influenza-like illness
finding_text: Among infected HCWs, 48.9% continued to work despite symptoms, leading to 234 exposed hospitalized patients; 137 received chemoprophylaxis and 2 patients developed laboratory-confirmed influenza after exposure
outcome_linked: nosocomial exposure from staff working while ill
key_limitations: ILI was included along with lab-confirmed influenza; descriptive design only
study_reported_implication: HCW presenteeism generated substantial patient exposure and downstream prophylaxis burden
codeblue_translation: Use staff presenteeism as an explicit vector-risk trigger requiring rapid review and exposure management

population: 278 healthcare workers with complete follow-up
acquisition_context: HCW infection burden relevant to nosocomial spread
hospital_acquired_definition: Not a patient HAI definition study; HCW influenza documented by PCR and/or seroconversion
risk_factor_definition: Cumulative influenza burden among healthcare workers including asymptomatic and paucisymptomatic infections
finding_text: Global cumulative influenza incidence was 22.3%; cumulative incidence was 10.4% for asymptomatic, 9.4% for paucisymptomatic, and 2.5% for symptomatic influenza among HCW
outcome_linked: silent and overt HCW influenza burden
key_limitations: Most diagnoses were identified through serology and systematic sampling rather than direct patient transmission tracing
study_reported_implication: Influenza burden among HCW was largely asymptomatic or paucisymptomatic
codeblue_translation: Use staff silent-burden estimates to justify universal preventive measures during influenza season

population: 278 healthcare workers with complete follow-up
acquisition_context: HCW infection burden relevant to nosocomial spread
hospital_acquired_definition: Not a patient HAI definition study; HCW influenza documented by PCR and/or seroconversion
comparator_group: ICU vs non-ICU staff work setting
risk_factor_definition: Working in an intensive care unit as a context associated with asymptomatic influenza among HCW
finding_text: In univariate analysis, working in an intensive care unit was associated with increased risk of asymptomatic influenza among HCW
clinical_context: ICU versus non-ICU hospital work setting
key_limitations: Crude association only; patient transmission was not directly measured
study_reported_implication: ICU work setting may harbor important silent HCW infection burden
codeblue_translation: Consider ICU staff as a potential silent-transmission group requiring consistent universal precautions

population: 90 infected HCWs responding to questionnaire
hospital_acquired_definition: Hospitalized patients exposed to HCWs with laboratory-confirmed influenza or ILI
risk_factor_definition: Self-reported reasons infected HCWs continued to work while ill
finding_text: The most common reasons for presenteeism were not feeling sick enough to take sick leave 40.0%, difficulty finding replacement HCWs 27.8%, and feeling it was unnecessary to take sick leave 16.7%
clinical_context: HCWs with influenza or influenza-like illness
key_limitations: Questionnaire-based study; included ILI alongside lab-confirmed influenza
study_reported_implication: Perceived mild illness and staffing constraints were the main drivers of presenteeism
codeblue_translation: Add mild-symptom presenteeism and staffing shortfall as explicit staff-vector governance risks

population: 278 healthcare workers with complete follow-up
acquisition_context: HCW infection burden relevant to nosocomial spread
hospital_acquired_definition: Not a patient HAI definition study; HCW influenza documented by PCR and/or seroconversion
comparator_group: HCW with influenza vs HCW without influenza
risk_factor_definition: Presence of three or more adults in the household as a context associated with asymptomatic influenza among HCW
finding_text: In univariate analysis, having >=3 adults in the household was associated with increased risk of asymptomatic influenza among HCW
key_limitations: Crude association only; patient transmission not directly measured
study_reported_implication: Household composition may contribute to silent HCW infection burden
codeblue_translation: Keep as low-priority contextual staff-vector signal rather than a core hospital predictor

population: 278 healthcare workers with complete follow-up
acquisition_context: HCW infection burden relevant to nosocomial spread
hospital_acquired_definition: Not a patient HAI definition study; HCW influenza documented by PCR and/or seroconversion
risk_factor_definition: Nurse professional role as a context associated with paucisymptomatic or symptomatic influenza among HCW
finding_text: In univariate analysis, nurse profession was associated with increased risk of paucisymptomatic or symptomatic influenza among HCW
key_limitations: Crude association only; no direct patient transmission tracing
study_reported_implication: Nurses may carry disproportionate clinically apparent influenza burden in hospital staff
codeblue_translation: Use nurse-role burden as an additional reason for nurse-focused prevention reinforcement
